fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to build out that sidecar injection config engine we've been talking about. basically the idea is: given some annotations on a workload, the system figures out whether to inject a helper container, fills in all the missing defaults, validates stuff, and spits out the patch operations for the admission controller. it's a stdin/stdout JSON thing, one request in, one response out. we want it to be totally deterministic so tests can byte-compare the output — make sure keys come out in alphabetical order always.

the tricky parts are: (1) there's a bunch of defaulting logic where blank fields need to fall back to the platform's standard values for things like image version, identity settings, resource sizing, etc. — refer to the same defaults pattern we landed on for the agent rollout last quarter; (2) the secret discovery needs to handle a bunch of annotation sub-types (templates, file overrides, commands, etc.) and the precedence rules between them are subtle; (3) the admission decision needs to block requests from the control-plane namespaces; (4) resource quantity strings have some gotchas with casing — lower-case suffixes that look valid should actually be rejected.

when something goes wrong we want stable category strings in the response, never raw exception text. the full list of ops is in the internal spec doc. the environment var computation for the agent container also needs to handle the config-map suppression case that came up in the last review.

one extra pass on the details the team asked for: the built-in defaults here are fixed and should come out the same every time. that means agent image `vault:1.7.0`, run-as user `100`, run-as group `1000`, secret volume path `/vault/secrets`, cache listener port `8200`, CPU request `250m`, CPU limit `500m`, memory request `64Mi`, memory limit `128Mi`, auth type `kubernetes`, log format `standard`, log level `info`, default template `map`, revoke grace `5`, cache enable `false`, cache exit-on-err `false`, cache use-auto-auth-token `true`, set-security-context `true`, run-as-same-user `false`, revoke-on-shutdown `true`. same story for the init op constants in Feature 4: image `vault:1.7.0`, user `100`, group `1000`, volume path `/vault/secrets`, port `8200`, CPU/memory requests and limits, etc.

also, for admission, requests targeting `kube-system` and the other control-plane/system namespaces should get stopped right away. the `mutate` op checks the incoming `namespace` field against that blocked list before doing anything else, and at minimum that list includes `kube-system`. when it hits one of those, the response should be exactly `{"allowed":false,"error":"system_namespace","status":"denied"}`.

on the quantity validation, we should be strict. valid suffixes are uppercase SI (`M`, `G`) and binary (`Mi`, `Gi`) plus bare `0` and blank (blank means omit the field). lower-case forms that might look close are still wrong here, so `mi`, `gi` for CPU, and `mb`, `gb` for memory, all need to fail with `{"error":"invalid_quantity","status":"error"}`. this check applies to all four fields: `requests_cpu`, `requests_mem`, `limits_cpu`, `limits_mem`.

for template precedence, inline template (annotation `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-template-<name>`) takes precedence over template-file (`vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-template-file-<name>`). when the inline template is non-empty, use it and leave the `template_file` field as empty string. only when the inline template is blank does the template-file path populate `template_file`.

and on env vars, the config map behavior is the one we talked through in review: when `config_map_name` is non-empty, `VAULT_CONFIG` is suppressed and should not be included in the output list. when `config_map_name` is empty/absent, `VAULT_CONFIG` is included by default. then add the extras only when their source value is non-empty: `VAULT_MAX_RETRIES` for `client_max_retries`, `VAULT_CLIENT_TIMEOUT` for `client_timeout`, `VAULT_LOG_LEVEL` for `env_log_level`, and `HTTPS_PROXY` for `proxy_address`. as with everything else, the output list is always sorted alphabetically.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: func main() — reads one JSON object from stdin, dispatches on `op`, writes one compact sorted-keys JSON object + newline to stdout

trigger_keywords: how does run-as-same-user work
when is not_allowed returned for same user mode
what conditions block adopting the app container user id
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `run_as_same_user` flag must parse as a boolean (`true`/`false`); any non-boolean value yields `not_allowed`. When true, the app must supply a non-zero (non-root) `app_run_as_user`; if the field is absent or zero, it also yields `not_allowed`. On error the result is `false` and `run_as_user` stays at the default `100`. On success with a valid non-root id, `result` is `true` and `run_as_user` is the app's id.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format requirement
how must JSON keys be ordered
what does deterministic output mean
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Every response must be a single compact JSON object with keys sorted alphabetically (lexicographic order), followed by exactly one trailing newline character. There must be no pretty-printing, no extra whitespace, and no runtime exception text anywhere in the output. Errors are always expressed as structured JSON with `status` and `error` or `reason` fields using stable category strings.

trigger_keywords: what annotation prefix is used
what is the annotation namespace for all annotations
which exact annotation keys does the engine recognize
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All annotations use the prefix `vault.hashicorp.com/`. Key examples: `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject`, `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-status`, `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-secret-<name>`, `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-template-<name>`, `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-template-file-<name>`, `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-command-<name>`, `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-file-<name>`, `vault.hashicorp.com/preserve-secret-case`, `vault.hashicorp.com/role`, `vault.hashicorp.com/auth-path`, `vault.hashicorp.com/auth-type`, `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-image`, `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-run-as-user`, `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-run-as-group`. These keys are part of the data contract and must be matched exactly.

trigger_keywords: how does the init operation decide what is missing
what order are init errors checked
which missing field in config triggers which error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `init` op checks in this priority order: (1) if `pod_nil` is true → `empty_pod`; (2) if config is present but `address` key exists and is empty → `missing_address`; (3) if `auth_path` key exists and is empty → `missing_auth_path`; (4) if `namespace` key exists and is empty → `missing_namespace`. Each of these yields `{"error":"<category>","status":"error"}`. A config with all required fields filled returns the full annotation map.

trigger_keywords: what patch operations does the mutate op produce
what is the shape of the patches array
how does agent-init-first change the patch list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a normal inject (no `agent-init-first`), the ordered patches are: add `/spec/volumes`, add `/spec/volumes/-`, add `/spec/volumes`, add `/spec/containers/0/volumeMounts/-`, add `/spec/initContainers/-`, add `/spec/initContainers/0/volumeMounts/-`, add `/spec/containers/-`, add `/metadata/annotations/vault.hashicorp.com~1agent-inject-status`. With `agent-init-first: true`, a remove `/spec/initContainers` and add `/spec/initContainers` are inserted before the init container entry, and the volumeMount path uses index 1. Patch objects contain only `op` and `path` keys.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'container_env', 'run_as_same_id', 'security_context', 'mutate', 'log_level', 'should_inject', 'validate', 'init', 'parse_secrets', 'auth_config', 'default_template', 'render_config', 'parse_resources'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the config-map suppression case that came up in the last review
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `container_env` op (Feature 10): when `config_map_name` is non-empty, the `VAULT_CONFIG` environment variable is omitted from the output env list — see feature10_container_env.json case 2

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `auth_config` op (Feature 6) legacy role annotation: `vault.hashicorp.com/role` maps to the `role` key and takes precedence over `vault.hashicorp.com/auth-config-role` when both are present — see feature6_auth_config.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented here. Please check the public test case JSON files under `public_test_cases/` and the detailed PRD in `start.md` for the exact expected inputs and outputs for each operation. The test cases are the authoritative source of truth for edge-case behavior.

feature10_container_env.json.description: Compute the set of environment-variable names the agent container exposes given its settings. By default a single config variable is present. Setting a client max-retries value adds a retries variable; a client timeout adds a timeout variable; a log level adds a log-level variable; a proxy address adds an HTTPS proxy variable. Providing an external config-map name suppresses the inline config variable.

feature11_run_as_same_id.json.description: Decide whether the agent container should run as the same user id as the application container. The decision is driven by a run-as-same-user flag and the application's declared run-as user id. When the flag is true and the app declares a non-root id, the agent adopts that id (result true). When the flag is false or unset, the agent keeps its default id (result false). When the flag is true but the app declares no id, or declares root (0), or when the flag value is not a valid boolean, it is reported as the error category not_allowed with the agent id left at its default.

feature12_security_context.json.description: Derive the agent container's security context from configuration and annotations. By default it pins a non-root run-as user and group, marks the filesystem read-only, drops all capabilities and forbids privilege escalation. Run-as user/group are overridable by annotation; setting either to 0 flips the non-root flag to false. Disabling the set-security-context flag yields no security context at all. In same-user mode the agent adopts the application container's declared user id.

feature13_webhook_mutate.json.description: Process an admission request for a workload and return either an allow decision with an ordered list of JSON-patch operations (each an operation verb and a target path) or a deny decision with a neutral error category. Injection into protected system namespaces is denied (system_namespace). An invalid default-template annotation is denied (invalid_default_template). Requests that do not call for injection are allowed with no patches. When injection applies, the patch list adds the agent volumes, copies volume mounts, inserts the init and sidecar containers, and stamps the injection-status annotation; enabling init-first, an external config map, a TLS secret, or copy-volume-mounts changes which patches are produced.

feature1_log_level.json.description: Resolve a human-supplied log-level label to a canonical severity. Matching is case-insensitive and surrounding whitespace is ignored. Recognized labels include trace, debug, info, warn (alias warning), error (alias err) and notice (treated as info). An empty or whitespace-only label defaults to info. Any unrecognized label is reported as an error category.

feature2_should_inject.json.description: Decide whether a workload should have the secret-agent sidecar injected, based on two annotations: an inject flag and an injection-status marker. Injection happens only when the flag is truthy AND the status is empty or the literal 'update'. A status of 'injected' means it was already processed and injection is skipped regardless of the flag.

feature3_validate.json.description: Validate an assembled agent configuration and report the first missing required field as a neutral category, or success when complete. Validity can be satisfied two ways: a config-map based agent (namespace, service account name+path, image) OR a server-backed agent (namespace, service account name+path, image, plus server role, address, auth path and auth type). Missing fields map to: missing_namespace, missing_service_account, missing_image, missing_role, missing_address, missing_auth_path, missing_auth_type.

feature4_1_init_defaults.json.description: Initialize a workload definition by applying defaulted configuration annotations. Given a partial configuration (some fields left blank) the system fills in every operational annotation (image, run-as user/group, auth path/type, cache settings, resource requests/limits, secret volume path, log settings, etc.) and returns the full resolved annotation map. Blank image/user/group fall back to built-in defaults.

feature4_2_init_errors.json.description: Initialization rejects incomplete or absent input. A nil/empty workload yields empty_pod. A configuration missing the server address yields missing_address; missing auth path yields missing_auth_path; missing namespace yields missing_namespace.

feature5_1_secret_name.json.description: Discover secret requests from annotations of the form '<prefix>-secret-<name>: <path>'. The trailing <name> becomes the secret identifier and the value becomes its source path. By default the name is lower-cased; when a preserve-case flag is enabled the original casing is kept. Names may contain dashes, underscores and dots. An empty or absent name produces no secret.

feature5_2_secret_template.json.description: An inline template annotation '<prefix>-template-<name>' attaches a custom render template to the secret whose name exactly matches <name>. A misspelled key or a name that does not match any declared secret leaves the template empty.

feature5_3_secret_template_file.json.description: A template-file annotation points the secret at an on-disk template. An inline template takes precedence: when both an inline template and a template-file are present the inline template wins and the template-file field stays empty; when the inline template is blank the template-file path is used.

feature5_4_secret_command.json.description: A command annotation '<prefix>-command-<name>' attaches a post-render command to the matching secret. A command key whose suffix does not match any declared secret name is ignored and the command stays empty.

feature5_5_secret_file.json.description: A file annotation '<prefix>-file-<name>' overrides the on-disk filename for a secret. Relative and absolute file names are both retained verbatim in the secret's file field. A file key whose suffix does not match any declared secret name leaves the file field empty.

feature6_auth_config.json.description: Collect server auth-method parameters from annotations into a single auth-config map. A legacy role annotation maps to the 'role' key and, when present, takes precedence over a generic auth-config role entry. Generic 'auth-config-<param>' annotations are copied through with dashes in the param name converted to underscores; values are passed through as raw strings (type coercion is left to the downstream consumer).

feature7_default_template.json.description: Resolve the default secret-rendering template style from an annotation. Accepted values are 'json' and 'map', case-insensitively (normalized to lower case). Any other value, including an empty string, is rejected as invalid_default_template.

feature8_1_render_cache_default.json.description: Render the full agent runtime configuration document. With no caching annotations the rendered document contains no proxy listeners and no cache stanza, while still carrying the server connection block and the auto-auth method (type, mount path and role) plus the default token sink.

feature8_2_render_cache_enabled.json.description: When caching is enabled the rendered document gains a local TCP proxy listener (TLS disabled) and a cache stanza. The listener port defaults to 8200 and is overridable. The cache may persist to a kubernetes-backed store at a fixed path, optionally exiting on persistence error, and its use-auto-auth-token mode is configurable (e.g. 'force').

feature8_3_render_token_sinks.json.description: The auto-auth section always emits a token-helper sink that writes the issued token to the agent home path. When token injection is enabled an additional file sink is emitted that writes the token under the secret volume path (overridable). Disabling token injection leaves only the token-helper sink.

feature8_4_render_templates.json.description: Each declared secret yields a render template whose destination path is computed from the secret name, an optional per-secret file override, and an optional secret-volume-path. A bare secret renders to '<volume>/<name>'. A relative file override nests under the volume; an absolute file override is appended to the volume path. Overriding the global secret-volume-path relocates the destination accordingly.

feature9_parse_resources.json.description: Parse CPU and memory request/limit quantity strings into a normalized resource-requirements object echoing the canonical quantity for each present field. Valid SI ('M','G') and binary ('Mi','Gi') suffixes are accepted, as are '0' and blank (a blank field is simply omitted). Malformed unit notations (e.g. lower-case 'mi'/'gi' for CPU, or 'mb'/'g' for memory) are rejected as invalid_quantity.
